topic_brief: Most robocalls never get reported anywhere, which is part of why enforcement lags behind the scale of the problem.
facts: Only about 1 in 32,571 robocalls received in 2025 were actually reported to the FTC.
92% of Americans have received an unwanted spam call at some point.
1,597,063 robocall complaints reached the FTC in 2025.
Two out of three Do Not Call Registry complaints in 2025 involved a robocall specifically.
stat_callout_label: robocalls received in 2025 actually got reported to the FTC

topic_brief: Reporting isn't a form letter into a void — the FTC, FCC, and FBI's IC3 each do something specific with a filed report.
facts: The FTC uses Do Not Call complaints to build cases against repeat violators and can fine companies that ignore the registry.
The FCC regulates telecom carriers directly and can require them to block traffic from providers that originate high volumes of illegal robocalls.
The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) aggregates reports to identify patterns and organized fraud operations across many victims.
A single report rarely stops a scammer immediately, but it becomes part of the evidence pattern regulators use to act.
stat_callout_label: one RoboCallMeNot log can guide you through reporting to

topic_brief: The sheer volume of robocalls placed nationwide, month over month.
facts: An estimated 4.2 billion robocalls were placed across the U.S. in April 2026 alone, per the YouMail Robocall Index.
That works out to roughly 13 robocalls for every person in the country in a single month.
Robocall volume has remained in the billions per month for several consecutive years despite ongoing carrier-level filtering efforts.
stat_callout_label: robocalls placed across the U.S. in a single month (April 2026)

topic_brief: The technical reason spoofed numbers work, explained simply for a non-technical reader.
facts: Caller ID was designed decades ago to display a number, not to verify who is actually calling.
STIR/SHAKEN is a newer carrier-level system that attaches a trust attestation to calls, and most major U.S. carriers now apply it to the large majority of their traffic.
Smaller, non-facilities-based carriers remain a gap scammers exploit to originate spoofed traffic.
'Neighbor spoofing' — displaying a number with your own area code and prefix — is a specific tactic designed to make you more likely to answer.
stat_callout_label: of major carrier traffic now carries a STIR/SHAKEN trust attestation

topic_brief: Why phone-first scams disproportionately cost older adults, without being condescending about it.
facts: Adults 50 and older reported $4.3 billion lost to fraud in 2025, compared with $2.3 billion for everyone younger, per FTC data.
Total fraud losses reported by adults 60+ climbed from about $600 million in 2020 to $2.4 billion in 2024.
Scams that start with a phone call carry a median reported loss of $2,210, versus $650 for scams that start on social media.
41% of imposter-scam reports involving losses of $10,000 or more from older adults in 2024 started with a phone call.
stat_callout_label: median reported loss when a scam starts with a phone call, vs. $650 on social media

topic_brief: The handful of scam scripts that show up again and again — auto warranty, tech support, government imposter, package delivery.
facts: Auto warranty robocalls have remained one of the most consistently reported robocall categories to the FTC for several years running.
Tech support scams, where a caller claims your computer is compromised, led to $159 million in reported losses among older consumers in 2024 alone.

Government imposter scams — callers claiming to be Social Security, the IRS, or law enforcement — rely on manufactured urgency and threats of immediate consequences, which real agencies do not do over the phone.
Package delivery and 'your order could not be shipped' scam calls and texts reliably spike around major shopping holidays.
stat_callout_label: scam scripts that account for a large share of reported robocalls, year after year

topic_brief: Making the case that logging even 'annoying but harmless' robocalls builds a useful pattern over time, not just the scary ones.
facts: Logging a call takes one tap and removes the main friction that keeps people from reporting: deciding in the moment whether a call was 'serious enough' to bother with.
stat_callout_label: is all it takes to log a call — RoboCallMeNot then walks you through filing it across agencies

topic_brief: How AI voice cloning has supercharged the classic 'grandparent scam,' and why hanging up and calling back on a known number matters more than ever.
facts: AI voice cloning tools can recreate a person's voice from as little as three seconds of audio, based on research first demonstrated in 2023.
The FBI's 2025 Internet Crime Complaint Center report logged more than 22,000 AI-related fraud complaints with losses exceeding $893 million.
In August 2025, federal prosecutors charged 13 people in a grandparent-scam ring that used cloned voices and stole more than $5 million from over 400 victims, whose average age was 84.
The FTC has warned specifically that scammers now use AI to make family-emergency scam calls sound convincingly like a real relative in distress.
The defense cybersecurity experts consistently point to: hang up and call the family member back directly on a number you already have, rather than trusting the incoming call.
stat_callout_label: of audio is enough for AI to convincingly clone someone's voice

topic_brief: The National Do Not Call Registry helps, but it has real, specific carve-outs people don't expect — worth knowing so the gaps don't feel like the registry 'isn't working.'
facts: The National Do Not Call Registry does not cover political calls, charities calling on their own behalf, or genuine survey calls — these are exempt by law.
A company you've done business with can still legally call you for up to 18 months after your last purchase, delivery, or payment, even if your number is registered.
If you've made an inquiry or application with a company, they're permitted to call you for up to 3 months afterward under the registry's rules.
Violating the registry can cost a telemarketer up to $53,088 per illegal call under current FTC penalty guidelines.
The FTC reported that consumer complaints about unwanted telemarketing calls fell more than 50% between 2021 and 2024.
stat_callout_label: drop in reported unwanted telemarketing calls from 2021 to 2024, per the FTC

topic_brief: The 'one-ring' or Wangiri scam — a call that rings once and hangs up, hoping curiosity makes you call back a number that charges you.
facts: The 'one-ring' scam, also called 'Wangiri' (Japanese for 'one and cut'), works by calling a number once and hanging up before it can be answered, hoping the recipient calls back out of curiosity.
The callback numbers used in these scams are often international premium-rate numbers that charge significant per-minute connection fees the moment the call connects.
The scam requires no personal information to be shared to cause financial harm — the callback itself is what generates the charge.
The FCC and FTC both advise that if you don't recognize a number that rang once and disconnected, the safest response is simply not to call it back.
stat_callout_label: is all it takes to trigger a Wangiri callback scam — the call never even needs to connect

topic_brief: Federal law already requires most automated sales calls to get written consent first — meaning almost every robocall you get is already illegal before it starts.
facts: Under the Telephone Consumer Protection Act, most automated telemarketing calls and texts require the recipient's prior express written consent before they can legally be sent.
Telemarketers are only permitted to call between 8 a.m. and 9 p.m. in the recipient's time zone under FTC telemarketing rules.
Callers are required to clearly identify themselves and the purpose of the call under the FTC's Telemarketing Sales Rule.
In March 2024, the FTC explicitly affirmed that AI-generated voices used in illegal robocalls are covered by these same existing consumer protection rules.
stat_callout_label: is the only legal window for telemarketing calls under FTC rules — most robocalls ignore it entirely
